questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this late-pregnancy change?
questions.scenario: A pregnant client with worsening hypertension reports severe right-upper-quadrant pain and nausea.
questions.additionalInfo: Platelets are falling, AST is elevated, and the client says the pain feels different from heartburn.

options.text: HELLP syndrome requiring urgent maternal-fetal escalation and hemorrhage-risk awareness
Expected indigestion late in pregnancy
A benign gallbladder problem without obstetric urgency
Normal musculoskeletal pain from uterine growth

questions.rationale: RUQ pain with falling platelets, liver injury, and worsening hypertension creates a HELLP map. The nurse should answer from maternal-fetal emergency risk, not from reflux or benign discomfort.

distractorRationales.b: Expected indigestion late in pregnancy misses the clinical pattern: Hellp Syndrome hypertension points to HELLP syndrome requiring urgent maternal-fetal escalation and hemorrhage-risk awareness, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: A benign gallbladder problem without obstetric urgency misses the clinical pattern: Hellp Syndrome hypertension points to HELLP syndrome requiring urgent maternal-fetal escalation and hemorrhage-risk awareness, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: Normal musculoskeletal pain from uterine growth misses the clinical pattern: Hellp Syndrome hypertension points to HELLP syndrome requiring urgent maternal-fetal escalation and hemorrhage-risk awareness, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: RUQ pain in preeclampsia stems is a clue you should not soften into heartburn.
questions.speedCue: Platelets down plus RUQ pain plus hypertension is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's next move for this client with sickle cell disease?
questions.scenario: A client admitted for a pain crisis now reports chest pain and increasing shortness of breath.
questions.additionalInfo: Temperature is 101.6 F (38.7 C), oxygen saturation has fallen to 88%, and breath sounds are diminished at the bases.

options.text: Acute chest syndrome requiring oxygenation-focused escalation and urgent treatment
A routine pain-crisis variation without pulmonary danger
Expected opioid sedation causing harmless shallow breathing
Simple anxiety because chest pain is common during crises

questions.rationale: Fever, chest pain, and falling oxygenation during a sickle-cell admission create an acute-chest map. The nurse should answer from pulmonary risk, not from generic pain-crisis assumptions.

distractorRationales.b: A routine pain-crisis variation without pulmonary danger misses the clinical pattern: Sickle Cell Acute Chest oxygen points to Acute chest syndrome requiring oxygenation-focused escalation and urgent treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Expected opioid sedation causing harmless shallow breathing misses the clinical pattern: Sickle Cell Acute Chest oxygen points to Acute chest syndrome requiring oxygenation-focused escalation and urgent treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Simple anxiety because chest pain is common during crises misses the clinical pattern: Sickle Cell Acute Chest oxygen points to Acute chest syndrome requiring oxygenation-focused escalation and urgent treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Once oxygenation falls, the question is no longer just about pain.
questions.speedCue: Sickle cell plus chest symptoms plus hypoxia is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this client's postoperative change?
questions.scenario: Several hours after thyroidectomy, a client becomes restless and develops high-pitched inspiratory stridor.
questions.additionalInfo: The neck dressing feels tight and the client is struggling to speak in full sentences.

options.text: Postoperative airway compromise requiring immediate airway support and urgent escalation
Expected throat irritation that only needs lozenges
Routine anxiety after surgery without true airway danger
Benign voice fatigue that can wait for the morning round

questions.rationale: Stridor and neck tightness after thyroidectomy create an airway-compromise map. The nurse should answer from ventilation rescue, not from comfort or reassurance.

distractorRationales.b: Expected throat irritation that only needs lozenges misses the clinical pattern: Thyroidectomy Stridor airway points to Postoperative airway compromise requiring immediate airway support and urgent escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Routine anxiety after surgery without true airway danger misses the clinical pattern: Thyroidectomy Stridor airway points to Postoperative airway compromise requiring immediate airway support and urgent escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Benign voice fatigue that can wait for the morning round misses the clinical pattern: Thyroidectomy Stridor airway points to Postoperative airway compromise requiring immediate airway support and urgent escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Stridor is the clue that ends the question quickly.

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's next move when this airway device comes out?
questions.scenario: A client with a tracheostomy placed 24 hours ago accidentally coughs the tube out during care.
questions.additionalInfo: The stoma is not mature and the client is showing rising respiratory effort.

options.text: Fresh-trach dislodgement requiring emergency airway escalation and no blind bedside replacement
Routine tube change that can be reinserted casually at bedside
A minor device issue that only needs the dressing adjusted
Expected coughing event without airway consequence

questions.rationale: A fresh trach that comes out creates an airway-emergency map because the tract is immature. The nurse should answer from airway rescue and escalation, not from routine mature-trach logic.

distractorRationales.b: Routine tube change that can be reinserted casually at bedside misses the clinical pattern: Tracheostomy Dislodgement airway points to Fresh-trach dislodgement requiring emergency airway escalation and no blind bedside replacement, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: A minor device issue that only needs the dressing adjusted misses the clinical pattern: Tracheostomy Dislodgement airway points to Fresh-trach dislodgement requiring emergency airway escalation and no blind bedside replacement, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Expected coughing event without airway consequence misses the clinical pattern: Tracheostomy Dislodgement airway points to Fresh-trach dislodgement requiring emergency airway escalation and no blind bedside replacement, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Fresh versus mature trach is the clue that changes the whole response.
questions.speedCue: A new trach coming out is the shortcut to emergency airway thinking.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move in this drainage-system problem?
questions.scenario: A client with a chest tube has continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber.
questions.additionalInfo: Breath sounds are stable and the nurse is deciding whether to ignore it, clamp the tube, or inspect the system.

options.text: Possible air leak requiring system inspection to identify where the leak is entering
Expected tidaling that means the system is definitely normal
A reason to clamp the chest tube immediately without further assessment
A harmless finding that never needs investigation

questions.rationale: Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber suggests an air leak until proven otherwise. The nurse should answer from system inspection and leak localization, not from passive reassurance or reflex clamping.

distractorRationales.b: Expected tidaling that means the system is definitely normal misses the clinical pattern: Chest Tube Air Leak safety points to Possible air leak requiring system inspection to identify where the leak is entering, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: A reason to clamp the chest tube immediately without further assessment misses the clinical pattern: Chest Tube Air Leak safety points to Possible air leak requiring system inspection to identify where the leak is entering, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: A harmless finding that never needs investigation misses the clinical pattern: Chest Tube Air Leak safety points to Possible air leak requiring system inspection to identify where the leak is entering, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Continuous bubbling and normal tidaling are not the same thing.
questions.speedCue: Water-seal continuous bubbling is the shortcut to air-leak thinking.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move for this client with heart failure?
questions.scenario: A client taking digoxin reports poor appetite, nausea, and new yellow halos around lights.
questions.additionalInfo: The apical pulse is 48/min and the client says the symptoms worsened after a week of poor oral intake.

options.text: Digoxin toxicity requiring the medication to be held and the provider notified
Expected vision change from aging eyes only
Routine heart-failure fatigue that should be ignored
Benign nausea unrelated to cardiac medication

questions.rationale: Yellow halos, nausea, poor intake, and bradycardia create a digoxin-toxicity pattern. The nurse should answer from medication danger, not from generic heart-failure symptoms.

distractorRationales.b: Expected vision change from aging eyes only misses the clinical pattern: Digoxin Toxicity bradycardia points to Digoxin toxicity requiring the medication to be held and the provider notified, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.c: Routine heart-failure fatigue that should be ignored misses the clinical pattern: Digoxin Toxicity bradycardia points to Digoxin toxicity requiring the medication to be held and the provider notified, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Benign nausea unrelated to cardiac medication misses the clinical pattern: Digoxin Toxicity bradycardia points to Digoxin toxicity requiring the medication to be held and the provider notified, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Visual change plus bradycardia is what makes this digoxin stem move fast.
questions.speedCue: Halos plus a slow pulse is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move for this home-dialysis client?
questions.scenario: A client performing peritoneal dialysis reports new abdominal pain during the drain phase.
questions.additionalInfo: The effluent is cloudy and the client has a temperature of 100.9 F (38.3 C).

options.text: Peritonitis requiring culture-focused escalation and treatment planning
Expected catheter discomfort that only needs reassurance
Routine dialysate temperature problem without infection concern
A harmless GI upset unrelated to dialysis

questions.rationale: Cloudy outflow, abdominal pain, and fever create a peritonitis map for PD. The nurse should answer from infection escalation, not from device irritation alone.

distractorRationales.b: Expected catheter discomfort that only needs reassurance misses the clinical pattern: Peritoneal Dialysis Peritonitis infection points to Peritonitis requiring culture-focused escalation and treatment planning, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Routine dialysate temperature problem without infection concern misses the clinical pattern: Peritoneal Dialysis Peritonitis infection points to Peritonitis requiring culture-focused escalation and treatment planning, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: A harmless GI upset unrelated to dialysis misses the clinical pattern: Peritoneal Dialysis Peritonitis infection points to Peritonitis requiring culture-focused escalation and treatment planning, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Cloudy dialysate is one of the fastest clues in PD stems.
questions.speedCue: Cloudy outflow plus pain should push infection to the top immediately.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this client's rapid deterioration?
questions.scenario: A client with chronic adrenal insufficiency has been vomiting for 24 hours and now looks weak and confused.
questions.additionalInfo: Blood pressure is 82/46 mm Hg, sodium is 124 mEq/L, potassium is 5.8 mEq/L, and the morning steroid dose was missed.

options.text: Adrenal crisis requiring immediate steroid replacement, fluid support, and shock-focused escalation
Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids
Expected fatigue from chronic illness without emergency risk
A benign stomach virus that can wait for clinic follow-up

questions.rationale: Vomiting, hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and missed steroids create an adrenal-crisis map. The nurse should answer from endocrine shock rescue, not from comfort care or delayed reassessment.

distractorRationales.b: Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids misses the clinical pattern: Adrenal Crisis Hypotension shock points to Adrenal crisis requiring immediate steroid replacement, fluid support, and shock-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Expected fatigue from chronic illness without emergency risk misses the clinical pattern: Adrenal Crisis Hypotension shock points to Adrenal crisis requiring immediate steroid replacement, fluid support, and shock-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: A benign stomach virus that can wait for clinic follow-up misses the clinical pattern: Adrenal Crisis Hypotension shock points to Adrenal crisis requiring immediate steroid replacement, fluid support, and shock-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: The lab pattern matters most when it arrives with hypotension and missed steroid coverage.
questions.speedCue: Hypotension plus low sodium plus high potassium should feel like adrenal crisis fast.

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's next move after this epidural change?
questions.scenario: Minutes after an epidural is placed, a laboring client becomes pale and dizzy.
questions.additionalInfo: Blood pressure drops to 84/48 mm Hg and the fetal heart tracing now shows recurrent late decelerations.

options.text: Maternal hypotension after epidural requiring perfusion rescue to protect fetal oxygenation
Expected epidural drowsiness with no fetal consequence
Placenta previa because the fetus shows late decelerations
Normal labor discomfort that only needs reassurance

questions.rationale: The pattern is maternal hypotension after epidural with immediate fetal late decelerations. The nurse should answer from maternal perfusion rescue, not from fetal monitoring alone.

distractorRationales.b: Expected epidural drowsiness with no fetal consequence misses the clinical pattern: Epidural Hypotension Fetal perfusion points to Maternal hypotension after epidural requiring perfusion rescue to protect fetal oxygenation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Placenta previa because the fetus shows late decelerations misses the clinical pattern: Epidural Hypotension Fetal perfusion points to Maternal hypotension after epidural requiring perfusion rescue to protect fetal oxygenation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Normal labor discomfort that only needs reassurance misses the clinical pattern: Epidural Hypotension Fetal perfusion points to Maternal hypotension after epidural requiring perfusion rescue to protect fetal oxygenation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: In labor, rescuing maternal blood pressure can be the fetal intervention.
questions.speedCue: Epidural plus hypotension plus late decels is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's response to this fetal tracing pattern?
questions.scenario: A laboring client receiving oxytocin develops recurrent late fetal heart rate decelerations.
questions.additionalInfo: The contraction pattern is frequent and strong, and the client is lying supine.

options.text: Uteroplacental perfusion compromise requiring intrauterine resuscitation and oxytocin interruption
A reassuring tracing that only needs continued observation
Normal fetal sleep variability without intervention need
A benign maternal discomfort pattern unrelated to the fetus

questions.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with strong oxytocin-driven contractions create a uteroplacental perfusion rescue map. The nurse should answer from intrauterine resuscitation, not from passive monitoring.

distractorRationales.b: A reassuring tracing that only needs continued observation misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue perfusion points to Uteroplacental perfusion compromise requiring intrauterine resuscitation and oxytocin interruption, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Normal fetal sleep variability without intervention need misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue perfusion points to Uteroplacental perfusion compromise requiring intrauterine resuscitation and oxytocin interruption, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: A benign maternal discomfort pattern unrelated to the fetus misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue perfusion points to Uteroplacental perfusion compromise requiring intrauterine resuscitation and oxytocin interruption, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Late decelerations are about placental perfusion, not just strip interpretation.
questions.speedCue: Oxytocin plus recurrent lates is the shortcut to resuscitation actions.

questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis or less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis.
questions.scenario: The client reports recurrent episodes of severe headache and pounding palpitations.

options.text: More consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis
Less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis

questions.rationale: Pheochromocytoma crisis is solved faster when the learner bundles headache, diaphoresis, palpitations, and severe hypertension as a catecholamine surge pattern. Chronic, non-episodic findings push the answer away from this diagnosis.

distractorRationales.a: More consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis misses the clinical pattern: Pheochromocytoma Crisis hypertension points to the row-specific answer pattern, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.b: Less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis misses the clinical pattern: Pheochromocytoma Crisis hypertension points to the row-specific answer pattern, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Headache, diaphoresis, palpitations, and severe episodic hypertension belong together as a pheochromocytoma pattern.
questions.speedCue: The speed clue is episodic sympathetic surge, not slow chronic symptom drift.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move for this client with kidney injury?
questions.scenario: A client with acute kidney injury reports weakness and nausea.
questions.additionalInfo: Telemetry shows peaked T waves and the potassium level is 6.8 mEq/L.

options.text: Hyperkalemia emergency requiring immediate cardiac stabilization and potassium-lowering treatment
Expected fatigue from hospitalization that only needs rest
Mild electrolyte change that can wait for the next lab draw
Simple dehydration without urgent membrane risk

questions.rationale: Peaked T waves plus marked hyperkalemia change this into a cardiac-stability emergency. The nurse should answer from conduction risk first, not from symptom management alone.

distractorRationales.b: Expected fatigue from hospitalization that only needs rest misses the clinical pattern: Hyperkalemia Ecg Emergency potassium points to Hyperkalemia emergency requiring immediate cardiac stabilization and potassium-lowering treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Mild electrolyte change that can wait for the next lab draw misses the clinical pattern: Hyperkalemia Ecg Emergency potassium points to Hyperkalemia emergency requiring immediate cardiac stabilization and potassium-lowering treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Simple dehydration without urgent membrane risk misses the clinical pattern: Hyperkalemia Ecg Emergency potassium points to Hyperkalemia emergency requiring immediate cardiac stabilization and potassium-lowering treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Telemetry changes are what make hyperkalemia urgent at the bedside.
questions.speedCue: Peaked T waves turn this from a lab problem into a rhythm problem.
